1: Eliphaz’s second speech intensifies the accusation: he begins by attacking Job’s speech itself as wind (v2), refusing to fear God (v4), and iniquity out of his own mouth (v5). In the NT, those who attack the righteous sufferer’s speech are typed in Christ’s accusers: the high priest’s question (“have you no answer to make?”, Mark 14:60), the mockers at the cross who used Jesus’s own words against him (Matthew 27:42–43). Eliphaz functions as the wisdom-tradition accuser whose confident theology has become a weapon against the innocent.
2: Eliphaz accuses Job of answering with windy knowledge (de’ah ruah) and filling his belly with east wind. In the NT, the opponents of the suffering Christ likewise charged that his words were without authority (John 7:15: “how does this man know letters, having never studied?”). The charge that suffering produces invalid theology is the perpetual accusation against those whose theology is shaped by genuine encounter with divine mystery rather than received tradition.
3: The question whether a righteous man should argue with unprofitable talk uses the wisdom tradition’s contempt for speech that produces nothing constructive. In contrast, Christ’s speech — even in its most suffering form — is productive: the seven last words from the cross are among the most theologically dense utterances in the NT. The tradition that dismisses suffering-speech as “unprofitable talk” is the tradition that failed to hear the divine voice in the cross.
4: Eliphaz’s charge that Job is undermining the fear of God (tafeer yir’ah) and hindering meditation before God inverts the reality: Job’s speech is a model of genuine encounter with God through suffering. In the NT, the high priests similarly accused Jesus of undermining religious devotion (John 11:48: “if we let him go on like this, everyone will believe in him”). The charge of undermining religion is regularly leveled against those who, like Job, challenge the comfortable theology that protects institutions from divine judgment.
5: Eliphaz accuses Job’s iniquity of teaching his mouth and his choosing the tongue of the crafty. In the NT, the scribes and Pharisees charged Jesus with speaking by the power of Beelzebul (Matthew 12:24) — attributing the genuine divine speech to demonic origin. The pattern: the accuser who cannot recognize God speaking through suffering must attribute the speech to wickedness or deception. The innocent sufferer’s speech is always misattributed by those whose theology has no category for innocent suffering.
6: Eliphaz says that Job’s own mouth condemns him, not Eliphaz. This anticipates the Pharisees’ attempt to catch Jesus in his own words (Luke 20:20: “spies who pretended to be sincere, that they might catch him in something he said”; Matthew 22:15: “snare him in his talk”). The tactic of using the sufferer’s own words as evidence of guilt is the oldest prosecutorial strategy in the book of Job and a recurring feature of Christ’s trial.
7: The rhetorical question — were you the first man born, were you brought forth before the hills? — challenges Job’s wisdom with a creation-priority argument. The NT answers this exact question about Christ: “He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation” (Colossians 1:15); “before Abraham was, I am” (John 8:58). The very question that Eliphaz uses to dismiss Job’s wisdom is the question that Christ answers affirmatively: yes, the pre-existent Logos was there before the hills.
8: The divine council question (“have you listened in the council of God and do you limit wisdom to yourself?”) ironically describes the very access that Christ, the eternal Son, has: Proverbs 8:22–31 describes Wisdom present at creation and daily beside the Creator. The prologue of Job (Job 1:6–12) shows that the real divine council has already decided Job’s case. Eliphaz’s accusation that Job claims divine-council access is simultaneously correct about Christ (who has full access) and wrong about Job (who is nonetheless right about his own innocence).
9: Eliphaz’s “what do you know that we do not know?” dismisses Job’s experiential knowledge. In the NT, the Pharisees’ dismissal of Jesus’ teaching with “who gave you this authority?” (Matthew 21:23) is the identical challenge. The wisdom that suffering produces — the knowledge of God’s faithfulness in darkness, the experiential theology of the cross — is precisely the knowledge that the comfortable wisdom tradition lacks.
10: The grey-haired and aged on Eliphaz’s side, older than Job’s father, represent the appeal to tradition as the final authority. Christ similarly faced the appeal to Mosaic tradition (John 9:28: “we are disciples of Moses”). The divine wisdom that speaks through suffering is consistently newer than the tradition that opposes it, yet it expresses what the tradition was always pointing toward without recognizing it.
11: Eliphaz offers the “consolations of God” (tanhumot ’el) as a gentle word. In 2 Corinthians 1:3–5, Paul describes the God who is “the Father of mercies and God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our affliction.” The true consolations of God come not through the friends’ theological lectures but through participation in the suffering of Christ (2 Corinthians 1:5: “as we share abundantly in Christ’s sufferings, so through Christ we share abundantly in comfort too”). Eliphaz offers consolation without participation; Christ offers consolation through participation.
12: The question “why does your heart carry you away, and why do your eyes flash?” accuses Job of emotional instability underlying his theology. The NT shows Jesus weeping at Lazarus’s tomb (John 11:35) and experiencing deep distress in Gethsemane (Matthew 26:37: “he began to be sorrowful and troubled”). The emotion of the sufferer before the mystery of death and divine hiddenness is not instability but depth of engagement with reality; Eliphaz’s charge of emotional instability is the accusation of those who have never faced what Job and Jesus faced.
13: Eliphaz’s charge that Job turns his spirit against God and lets such words go out of his mouth anticipates the passion narrative accusation of blasphemy. Mark 14:64: the high priest says “you have heard his blasphemy” after Jesus’s claim to be seated at the right hand of Power. The religious establishment’s category for the sufferer’s honest speech to God is blasphemy; the NT will show that what appeared to be blasphemy was in fact the Son speaking truthfully about himself.
14: “What is man that he can be pure, or he who is born of a woman that he can be righteous?” — this rhetorical question is the theological heart of the anthropological problem that the incarnation answers. The incarnate Christ, “born of a woman, born under the law” (Galatians 4:4), is the one born of woman who is fully righteous. Matthew 11:11 uses the identical phrase “born of women” (“among those born of women there has arisen no one greater than John the Baptist”) precisely to mark the category that Christ transcends. Eliphaz’s rhetorical question has a real answer: the one born of a virgin who is simultaneously fully God.
15: “Behold, God puts no trust even in his holy ones, and the heavens are not pure in his sight” — this theology of universal impurity before God is the backdrop against which Christ’s unique purity stands out. Hebrews 7:26–27: “it was fitting that we should have a high priest, holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, exalted above the heavens. He has no need, like those high priests, to offer sacrifices daily, first for his own sins and then for those of the people.” Christ is the exception to Eliphaz’s universalized impurity: the one mortal born of woman who is truly pure.
16: “How much less one who is abominable and corrupt, a man who drinks injustice like water” — the double descent of v15–16 (even the holy ones/heavens are impure, so how much less corrupt humanity) uses the same logic as the divine speeches (Job 40:19: the creature is dwarfed by the Creator). The NT applies this argument in reverse for Christ: if even holy ones are impure before God, and Christ is purer than all — then Christ is the singular exception who surpasses the entire creaturely order.
17: Eliphaz’s offer to show Job wisdom he has seen uses the posture of the authoritative teacher passing down traditional wisdom. The Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:21: “you have heard that it was said to those of old…but I say to you”) inverts this: Christ speaks not from tradition but from divine authority. The contrast between Eliphaz teaching from the tradition of the fathers and Christ teaching as the one who establishes what the fathers were pointing toward is the structural contrast between law-wisdom and incarnate Wisdom.
18: The wisdom that “wise men have told, and their fathers have not hidden” is the received tradition that Eliphaz appeals to. In the NT, Paul describes “the mystery hidden for ages and generations but now revealed to his saints” (Colossians 1:26) — the wisdom that was not fully visible in the tradition, hidden in types and figures, now disclosed in Christ. Eliphaz’s appeal to what has always been taught is precisely the category that cannot contain the new thing God is doing in the incarnation.
19: The appeal to those “to whom alone the land was given, and no stranger passed among them” is an ethnic purity argument: the pure wisdom lineage of the patriarchal age. The NT explicitly breaks this: the gospel is for all nations (Matthew 28:19; Ephesians 3:6: “the Gentiles are fellow heirs, members of the same body”). The Christological revelation is precisely the revelation to the nations that the “land-restricted” ethnic wisdom could not have anticipated.
20: The wicked man writhing in pain all his days begins the long description of the wicked person’s misery (vv20–35). This description functions as the mirror-image of the Suffering Servant: the wicked person suffers for his own sin (Eliphaz’s claim about Job); the Servant suffers not for his own sin but for others (Isaiah 53:4–5). The verses describing the wicked are typologically significant not as direct prophecy but as structural contrast: every element of the wicked one’s misery that Eliphaz applies to Job will ultimately be borne by Christ on behalf of the truly wicked.
21: Terrifying sounds in the wicked man’s ears, a destroyer coming on him in prosperity anticipates the sudden arrest of Christ at Gethsemane: in the middle of a week of triumphal entry and teaching, the destroyer came suddenly. Luke 22:53: “this is your hour and the power of darkness.” The terror that the wicked man carries through his days is the terror that Christ voluntarily entered in Gethsemane, knowing what was coming.
22: The wicked man not believing he will return from darkness, destined for the sword anticipates Christ’s voluntary entry into death: “he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross” (Philippians 2:8). Unlike the wicked man who cannot escape the sword, Christ freely submitted to it. The darkness he did not believe he would escape is the darkness Christ entered and conquered (John 1:5: “the light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it”).
23: The wicked wandering for bread, knowing the day of darkness is ready at hand, resonates with the one who voluntarily became poor (2 Corinthians 8:9: “though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich”). Christ identified with the wandering poor; the day of darkness — the crucifixion — was the day he had been moving toward since Bethlehem.
24: Distress and anguish terrifying the wicked like a king ready to attack uses military language that Gethsemane embodies: “my soul is very sorrowful, even to death” (Matthew 26:38). Christ entered the distress and anguish of facing the full weight of divine judgment — not because he was wicked, but because he bore the wicked’s penalty. The anguish that the wicked deserve for their own sin, Christ experienced on their behalf.
25: The wicked man stretching out his hand against God, running defiantly against the Almighty, is the picture of human rebellion that reaches its climax in the crucifixion: the chief priests and crowd who cried “crucify him” were in this deepest sense stretching their hands against God incarnate. Acts 4:26–28 applies Psalms 2:2 (“the kings of the earth set themselves against the LORD”) to the events of the crucifixion: the gathering of Herod, Pontius Pilate, the Gentiles, and Israel against God’s anointed was the supreme instance of running against the Almighty.
26: Running against God with a thick-bossed shield describes aggressive defiance of the divine. At Calvary, the mockers under the cross “derided him, wagging their heads” (Mark 15:29) — the ancient gesture of aggression and contempt. The human boldness before the apparently defeated Christ on the cross was the ultimate expression of the wicked man’s deluded confidence in his own strength against God.
27: The wicked man with a fat face, gathering fat on his waist, describes physical prosperity fueling spiritual complacency. Luke 12:19–20 (the rich fool with barns full of goods): “Soul, you have ample goods laid up for many years; relax, eat, drink, be merry’—But God said to him, ‘Fool! This night your soul is required of you.’” The fat-gathered prosperity becomes the occasion of sudden divine demand — the same pattern Eliphaz describes.
28: The wicked living in desolate cities, in houses no one should inhabit, ready to become heaps of rubble anticipates the “desolation” language of the eschatological discourse (Matthew 24:15: “the abomination of desolation”; Luke 21:20: “when you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, then know that its desolation has come near”). The ruins that the wicked inhabit are the ultimate expression of the city that rejects God’s messenger (Matthew 23:38: “your house is left to you desolate”).
29: The wicked not growing rich, with his wealth not enduring, not extending his possessions over the earth, anticipates the NT pattern of the rich who will lose everything: “do not lay up for yourselves treasures on earth” (Matthew 6:19); “the cares of the world and the deceitfulness of riches choke the word” (Matthew 13:22). Christ himself had nowhere to lay his head (Luke 9:58) as the deliberate embodiment of the non-accumulation that the wicked fail to practice by choice.
30: The wicked not escaping darkness, the flame withering his shoots, his blossom blown away by the wind of God’s mouth uses vegetation metaphors for destroyed potential. John 15:6: “whoever does not abide in me is thrown away like a branch and withers; and the branches are gathered, thrown into the fire, and burned.” The divine wind/breath that blows away the wicked man’s shoots is the same divine judgment that the final separation accomplishes — the withering of those who do not remain in Christ, the true Vine.
31: The warning not to trust in emptiness and be deceived (self-deception leading to worthlessness) anticipates Christ’s warning about false prophets (Matthew 7:15–16: “beware of false prophets…you will recognize them by their fruits”) and false security (Matthew 7:26: “everyone who hears these words of mine and does not do them will be like a foolish man who built his house on the sand”). The deception that produces emptiness is the anti-type of the truth that produces fruit (John 15:5).
32: The wicked man filled before his time, his branch not green, uses the premature-ending metaphor for a life that was directed toward false ends. Christ’s death, by contrast, appeared to be a premature ending but was actually the fulfillment of purpose: John 12:24: “unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit.” The ending that looked like failure was the precondition for the fruit that now fills the world.
33: The wicked like a vine casting off unripe grapes, shaking off blossoms like an olive tree describes unsuccessful fruiting — the tree that looks productive but produces nothing edible. Mark 11:12–14 (Jesus cursing the fig tree with leaves but no fruit) applies this exact image to Israel’s religious establishment: the appearance of fruitfulness (leaves/blossoms) with no actual fruit. The cursed fig tree that withers is the NT’s anti-type to Christ as the true Vine and true fruit-producer.
34: The congregation of the godless being barren, fire consuming the tents of bribery, anticipates the judgment against the religious establishment that opposed Jesus: Matthew 23:38 (“your house is left to you desolate”); Luke 19:43–44 (Jerusalem’s destruction). The fire that consumes the tents of those who corrupt justice is the judgment that Jerusalem’s rejection of the Son brought upon it in 70 CE.
35: Conceiving trouble and bringing forth evil, with their belly preparing deceit, describes the full cycle of sin from internal conception to external expression (cf. James 1:15: “sin when it is fully grown brings forth death”). The contrast with Christ is complete: he conceived no sin, brought forth no evil, and prepared no deceit (1 Peter 2:22: “he committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth”; Isaiah 53:9). Job’s friends have applied to him the description that belongs only to those who are genuinely guilty; the one to whom the description truly does not apply is Christ.
